query: Why does Vue recommend the Composition API over mixins today?

competitors.label: Composition API FAQ
competitors.selectionReason: This is the strongest official Vue page for why Composition API exists, how it improves logic organization, and why mixins are no longer the preferred reuse model.
theirStrengths: The official FAQ is authoritative on motivation, trade-offs, coexistence with Options API, and long-term design guidance.
theirGaps: It is broader and less compressed around the interview-specific mixins-versus-composables decision.

realWorldUseCases: mixins scale poorly: they hide where logic/state comes from, can collide/override silently, and are hard to type and refactor.

Composition API fixes that by making reuse explicit (import + call a composable), composable (combine multiple functions safely), and TypeScript-friendly (inference works through function signatures).
followUpConfusion: If multiple sources define the same key, you can get overrides or merged behavior (hooks).
you read a component and can’t confidently tell what runs, in what order, and which definition “wins”.

realWorldUseCases: The FAQ already frames Composition API around scaling logic organization, composables, and type inference.
actionableExamples: The official page includes useful examples and migration framing, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: The docs explain design trade-offs well, but our page is more direct on collision, override, and hidden-source debugging follow-ups.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one shorter composable extraction example to make the before-versus-after contrast even faster to scan.

competitors.winReason: The official FAQ is broader on framework rationale, but our page is stronger as a compact interview answer because it centers mixin collisions, hidden dependencies, and the explicit composable alternative.

competitors.label: Vue Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's Vue interview bank is directly relevant because mixins, composables, and reuse patterns are core Vue interview themes.
theirStrengths: It is a strong Vue interview-prep hub with broad category coverage.
theirGaps: The category page is broader than this exact mixins-versus-composables architecture question.

realWorldUseCases: Composition API approach: explicit, local, and composable
Practical scenario You want to reuse form validation across multiple Vue components, choosing between a composable or a mixin.
followUpConfusion: Naming is explicit (and renameable), collisions are controlled, and types are predictable.
Hidden dependencies in mixins that are hard to trace.

realWorldUseCases: The bank is relevant at the topic level, but it is less focused on this exact reuse trade-off.
actionableExamples: GreatFrontEnd remains competitive on worked interview examples, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: The category page is less explicit about action-at-a-distance, merge behavior, and why composables reduce those follow-up problems.

actionableExamples: Keep the answer short enough that the practical scenario remains visible instead of getting buried under architecture prose.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is useful as a Vue interview hub, but our page is stronger because it answers the exact mixins-versus-Composition-API question directly and ties it to real refactor pain.

competitors.label: What is a Vue.js Composable?
competitors.selectionReason: Vue School has a topic-direct article that explicitly positions composables as the Composition API answer to the same reuse problems that mixins historically solved.
theirStrengths: It is a direct tutorial on composables and why they are preferable for reusable logic.
theirGaps: It is less tightly focused on the hidden merge behavior and collision footguns that make mixins age poorly.

realWorldUseCases: Composition API fixes that by making reuse explicit (import + call a composable), composable (combine multiple functions safely), and TypeScript-friendly (inference works through function signatures).
With composables, logic reuse is just function composition: you import what you need, call it, and use returned refs/functions.
followUpConfusion: Mixins merge into the component options.
The result: you read a component and can’t confidently tell what runs, in what order, and which definition “wins”.

realWorldUseCases: The article is directly aligned on composables and reuse, so the use-case comparison is close.
actionableExamples: Vue School remains competitive on practical code examples, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is stronger on the exact follow-up an interviewer asks next: what breaks when multiple mixins merge into one component.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one smaller rename-on-destructure example to make collision avoidance even more concrete.

competitors.winReason: Vue School is a strong composables tutorial, but our page is stronger on the specific reasons Vue moved away from mixins in real codebases: hidden APIs, merge behavior, and collision-driven refactor pain.
